text: Carbohydrate ingestion during prolonged (>2 h) moderate-to-high intensity exercise significantly improves endurance performance
For prolonged exercise lasting 2-3 hours, athletes should ingest carbohydrates at a rate of 60 g per hour to allow for maximal exogenous glucose oxidation rates
Well-trained endurance athletes competing longer than 2.5 hours can metabolize up to 90 g/h of carbohydrate when multiple transportable carbohydrates are ingested (e.g. glucose plus fructose)

Small amounts of carbohydrate ingestion can enhance performance during shorter (45-60 min), high-intensity (>75% VO2peak) exercise despite adequate endogenous carbohydrate stores, likely via central nervous system mechanisms
For rapid post-exercise glycogen repletion, athletes should consume 1.2 g/kg/h of carbohydrate during the acute recovery phase after exhaustive exercise
population: athletes requiring rapid recovery within 24 h

text: Co-ingesting protein (0.2-0.4 g/kg/h) with less carbohydrate (0.8 g/kg/h) can achieve similar glycogen repletion rates as high carbohydrate alone, which is useful for athletes with gastrointestinal tolerance issues post-exercise
population: athletes with lower GI tolerance post-exercise

text: The ergogenic effects of carbohydrate during prolonged exercise are likely related to skeletal muscle glycogen sparing, prevention of liver glycogen depletion and hypoglycemia, and maintaining high carbohydrate oxidation rates
